first_checks: Probe soil moisture inside and outside the affected area
Inspect the pattern at sunrise and late afternoon
Check whether symptoms respond quickly to hand watering
confirmatory_signs: Fast rebound after hand watering favors moisture stress
Distinct lesions or mycelium point toward disease
Chronic midday collapse with shallow roots suggests rootzone stress
avoid_assumptions: Do not default to fungicides before checking moisture and roots.
Wilt, LDS, and anthracnose can overlap on stressed greens.
wilt_vs_disease_on_greens.escalation: Pull cup-cutter plugs and inspect roots, layering, black layer, and organic matter if the pattern keeps returning.

first_checks: Note whether the ring is dry, greasy, smoke-ring like, or mushroom-associated
Probe soil moisture in the ring and center
Inspect for hydrophobicity, mycelium, or fruiting bodies
confirmatory_signs: Hydrophobic dry ring and water repellency support fairy ring or LDS interaction
Smoke-ring margins with humid overnight weather support brown patch
Mushrooms or recurring arcs in the same area support fairy ring
avoid_assumptions: Not every ring is a fairy ring.
Moisture behavior often matters as much as symptom color.
ring_pattern_diagnostics.escalation: Use a soil surfactant and physical probing data alongside disease observations before choosing chemistry.

first_checks: Pull plugs to inspect root depth, black layer, layering, and odor
Assess moisture distribution through the upper profile
Compare roots in healthy versus declining turf
confirmatory_signs: Shallow roots across a broad area suggest cultural or physical limits
Black or sulfur-smelling layers point toward anaerobic conditions
Patchy blackened roots with disease-favorable weather may support root pathogens
avoid_assumptions: Color response after fertilizer does not prove the root system is healthy.
Watering more can worsen anaerobic rootzones.
rootzone_failure_framework.escalation: If roots repeatedly fail despite reasonable moisture, investigate organic matter, drainage, layering, and oxygen status.

first_checks: Map the spray pattern, overlaps, turn areas, and drift direction
Check timing of symptoms relative to the application
Compare affected species and unaffected species nearby
confirmatory_signs: Sharp equipment patterns, overlap zones, or one-species selectivity support spray injury
Twisting, whitening, stunting, or distorted growth can help narrow herbicide mode of action
Uniform edge burn after application may fit fertilizer or spray carrier issues
avoid_assumptions: Do not call it disease if the pattern matches the spray rig.
Do not ignore tank-mix additives, heat, and drought at application time.
herbicide_injury_framework.escalation: Review the full spray log, rate, carrier, adjuvant, weather, and tank-cleanout history.

first_checks: Review recent PGR timing, rate, interval, and growing degree day assumptions
Assess whether turf was already stressed before application
Compare treated and untreated zones if available
confirmatory_signs: Excess bronzing, tight suppression, or delayed rebound after aggressive intervals suggests over-regulation
Traffic and low nitrogen can amplify the appearance of PGR injury
Healthy suppression should still leave acceptable density and recovery
avoid_assumptions: A slower-growing canopy is not automatically a problem.
But severe suppression on already-stressed turf can become a real injury event.
pgr_stress_framework.escalation: Reset interval strategy, review clipping yield trends, and support recovery with moisture and nutrition rather than reflexively reapplying.

first_checks: Compare entrances, exits, turning areas, and high-footfall lines against symptom maps
Probe firmness and depth of rooting in traffic and non-traffic areas
Review cart and foot-traffic patterns
confirmatory_signs: Linear wear patterns and repeated weak lanes support traffic
Shallow roots and sealed surfaces support compaction
Recovery after rerouting traffic supports the diagnosis
avoid_assumptions: Do not keep blaming disease when the traffic map tells the story.
Traffic, moisture, and compaction often stack together.
traffic_compaction_decline.escalation: Pair cultivation, fertility, and traffic-control changes instead of trying to solve chronic wear with one input.

first_checks: Inspect crowns and lower stems for basal blackening, thinning, and weak tillers
Review recent nitrogen pace, mowing intensity, rolling frequency, and traffic concentration
Compare Poa annua-rich zones versus bentgrass-rich zones within the same surface
confirmatory_signs: Basal collapse on stressed low-cut turf supports anthracnose decline
Poa annua often shows more severe damage than surrounding bentgrass
Symptoms align with low fertility, heat, traffic, and sustained conditioning pressure
avoid_assumptions: Do not treat anthracnose as a spray-only issue when the turf is under stacked stress.
Do not assume every thinning Poa patch is simple wilt.
anthracnose_decline_framework.escalation: If the crown is collapsing, reduce stress, stabilize nutrition and moisture, and confirm whether labeled anthracnose materials are part of the plan.

first_checks: Compare moisture readings with EC or salinity readings in healthy and affected turf
Review irrigation-water source, leaching history, and drainage performance
Inspect root depth and whether wilt persists despite apparently adequate water
confirmatory_signs: High EC with persistent wilt despite moisture supports osmotic stress
Marginal burn, poor recovery, and reclaimed-water history strengthen the salinity diagnosis
Rapid rebound after true leaching supports salts more than simple drought
avoid_assumptions: Do not assume wet soil means the plant can use the water.
Do not chase osmotic stress with endless light irrigation.
salt_vs_drought_framework.escalation: Collect EC, sodium, and water-quality data before changing the entire irrigation schedule.

bermuda_spring_transition_failure.problem_space: Slow bermudagrass spring transition and patch recovery
first_checks: Map dead patches, weak margins, low spots, and shaded or compacted zones
Check whether bermudagrass spread is occurring from healthy edges
Review winter injury, drainage, and fall growth history
confirmatory_signs: Circular dead patches and slow edge spread support spring dead spot injury
Cold, wet, or shaded areas recover later and often stay thin longer
Dead centers do not suddenly green up; recovery comes from surrounding stolons
avoid_assumptions: Do not call spring lag a fertility problem before checking temperature, drainage, and winter injury.
Do not force weak bermuda with aggressive practices too early.
bermuda_spring_transition_failure.escalation: Build the recovery plan around bermuda spread, traffic protection, and next-season fall prevention.

warm_season_slow_greenup_framework.problem_space: Slow warm-season green-up or uneven spring response
first_checks: Compare soil temperature, shade, moisture, and thatch in good and slow areas
Map whether the lag is species-specific, shade-specific, or profile-specific
Review winter injury, fall management, and early spring traffic
confirmatory_signs: Uniform species-specific lag with cool soils supports normal thermal delay more than disease
Dead centers with slow edge spread support winter injury or spring dead spot
Shaded, wet, or thatchy areas staying behind support environmental delay
avoid_assumptions: Do not assume every slow green-up needs more fertilizer.
Do not diagnose disease before checking temperature, shade, and winter history.
warm_season_slow_greenup_framework.escalation: If green-up stays uneven after soils warm, inspect crowns, stolons, and drainage to separate temperature lag from true injury.

first_checks: Pull recent irrigation-water analysis for EC, sodium, chloride, bicarbonates, and alkalinity
Compare symptoms on the most heavily irrigated or poorly leached areas
Review whether the site is using reclaimed water or a changing source
confirmatory_signs: Stress maps with irrigation intensity or low-leaching zones support a water-quality driver
Chlorosis, marginal burn, or persistent wilt with chemistry abnormalities strengthen the diagnosis
Surface response changes when the water source or leaching conditions change
avoid_assumptions: Do not guess from canopy color alone when water chemistry data is missing.
Do not separate nutrient and salt effects if the source water is driving both.
water_quality_chemistry_framework.escalation: Use water, soil, and infiltration data together before changing fertility, amendment, or acidification programs.

first_checks: Confirm where and how samples were pulled: healthy, transition, and decline zones
Compare root depth, root quality, and current stress at the time of sampling
Review species identity and counts rather than reading the report as a simple positive/negative
confirmatory_signs: Damaging species plus weak roots and a matching field pattern strengthen the diagnosis
Borderline counts matter more when heat, drought, or traffic are already stressing the turf
Symptomatic and healthy-area sample differences are often more useful than one isolated count
avoid_assumptions: Do not treat every nematode report as the primary cause without field context.
Do not dismiss moderate counts if the root system and site history support the diagnosis.
nematode_sampling_interpretation_framewo.escalation: If counts and symptoms line up, plan around root recovery and repeated sampling rather than chasing one fast visual fix.

herbicide_carryover_framework.problem_space: Residual herbicide carryover and establishment failure
first_checks: Review full spray history, rates, intervals, rainfall, and reseeding timeline
Map whether poor establishment matches application zones more than irrigation or fertility zones
Compare seedling injury with mature turf response nearby
confirmatory_signs: Seedlings are suppressed or distorted where residual chemistry history is strongest
The problem repeats after the same transition or overseeding plan
Mature turf looks less affected than new seedlings or transition grass
avoid_assumptions: Do not call it poor seed before checking the residual chemistry history.
Do not rely on generic calendar timing when site conditions may have stretched persistence.
herbicide_carryover_framework.escalation: Use label intervals, site conditions, and species sensitivity together before changing the next reseeding plan.

insect_feeding_pattern_framework.problem_space: Insect feeding pattern versus stress look-alikes
first_checks: Open the turf, thatch, stems, or soil to confirm the insect and feeding site
Review whether activity is foliar, crown/stem, or root feeding
Compare symptom timing with known life-cycle windows and recent weather
confirmatory_signs: The actual insect or feeding damage is found where the pattern is strongest
Symptoms match the insect's feeding site and current life stage
The timing aligns with expected activity, not just with general summer stress
avoid_assumptions: Do not call it insect damage without finding the insect or clear feeding evidence.
Do not let animal activity alone stand in for the real diagnosis.
insect_feeding_pattern_framework.escalation: If the pattern is ambiguous, return with stage-specific scouting instead of forcing a product decision from appearance alone.

species_fit_renovation_framework.problem_space: Species-fit failure versus tactical management issue
first_checks: Map where the turf repeatedly fails and compare that to shade, traffic, water, and mowing pressure
Review whether a better-adapted species is already outperforming nearby under the same conditions
Compare the current rescue burden against the surface expectations and region
confirmatory_signs: The same species repeatedly fails in the same context over multiple seasons
Another species or conversion area performs better under the same constraints
The current stand depends on chronic rescue inputs to stay acceptable
avoid_assumptions: Do not jump to renovation from one stressful season.
Do not keep calling it bad luck if the site keeps proving the same fit problem.
species_fit_renovation_framework.escalation: If the same failure pattern keeps repeating, compare phased renovation against another season of rescue management.

overseeded_transition_competition_framew.problem_space: Overseeded ryegrass hanging on too long during spring transition
first_checks: Map where ryegrass stays dominant versus where bermudagrass is actually spreading
Review spring nitrogen, irrigation frequency, shade, and traffic pressure
Compare soil warmth and compaction in the slowest-transition zones
confirmatory_signs: Ryegrass remains dense while bermudagrass only advances in the warmest or least-trafficked areas
Transition stalls after cool weather, frequent irrigation, or nitrogen that favors ryegrass persistence
The weakest recovery zones are also the slowest to warm or the most compacted
avoid_assumptions: Do not blame transition entirely on the overseed if bermudagrass recovery conditions are still poor.
Do not chase cosmetic spring color if it keeps strengthening ryegrass competition.
overseeded_transition_competition_framew.escalation: Build the transition plan around bermudagrass spread, not just ryegrass removal speed.

seedling_establishment_failure_framework.problem_space: Good germination followed by weak establishment or stand collapse
first_checks: Check rooting depth, seed-soil contact, and moisture by depth rather than only surface appearance
Map whether failure follows wet low spots, crusting, sealing, traffic, or irrigation pattern
Review salinity, residual chemistry, and early traffic timing if the seedbed picture is not enough
confirmatory_signs: Initial emergence looks acceptable, then density drops as shallow roots fail to advance
Seedlings pull easily or remain rooted only in the very top layer
Failure lines up with wetness, crusting, sealing, or early traffic rather than random seed distribution
avoid_assumptions: Do not call it poor seed without checking the early rooting environment.
Do not keep the seedbed constantly wet if oxygen and rooting are already compromised.
seedling_establishment_failure_framework.escalation: Treat the problem as early-survival failure and review seedbed oxygen, traffic exclusion, and water pattern together.

application_pattern_coverage_framework.problem_space: Application pattern, streaking, or uneven coverage issue
first_checks: Map whether the symptom follows passes, skips, overlap bands, or edge-of-swath geometry
Review nozzle type, pressure, speed, boom height, and carrier volume from the application
Use pattern checks or water-sensitive paper before changing the chemistry assumption
confirmatory_signs: The symptom pattern follows application geometry more closely than topography or irrigation
Coverage checks show visible non-uniformity or overlap
Performance changes after setup adjustments without changing the active ingredient
avoid_assumptions: Do not call it resistance first if the field map looks like application geometry.
Do not change products before checking the delivery pattern.
application_pattern_coverage_framework.escalation: Treat it as an application-quality problem until pattern verification proves otherwise.

mechanical_stress_budget_framework.problem_space: Cumulative mechanical stress from mowing, rolling, and tournament pressure
first_checks: List current mowing, rolling, traffic, and event-conditioning intensity together
Compare recovery signals on the most stressed greens against the strongest greens
Review clipping yield, wilt, rebound, and canopy elasticity during the stress period
confirmatory_signs: Symptoms intensify as rolling and conditioning pressure stack up
Backing off mechanical intensity improves resilience faster than changing chemistry alone
The highest-speed or highest-traffic surfaces show the problem first
avoid_assumptions: Do not treat rolling as mechanically free just because it improves speed.
Do not read every tournament decline as disease before checking stress stacking.
mechanical_stress_budget_framework.escalation: Build the response around total stress reduction and recovery margin, not just one isolated practice.

cut_quality_leaf_shredding_framework.problem_space: Poor cut quality, leaf shredding, and false disease alarms
first_checks: Inspect leaf tips with a hand lens right after mowing
Review reel, bedknife, and height-of-cut setup on the affected unit
Compare whether the symptom follows mowing timing more than weather or spray timing
confirmatory_signs: Leaf tips are frayed or torn instead of cleanly cut
Discoloration appears immediately after mowing or on the most aggressively cut surfaces
Cut-quality correction improves the symptom without a major chemistry change
avoid_assumptions: Do not diagnose foliar disease from shredded leaf tips alone.
Do not keep chasing chemistry if cut quality is visibly poor.
cut_quality_leaf_shredding_framework.escalation: Correct the cut first, then reassess whether any remaining symptom still points to disease or stress.

topdressing_program_drift_framework.problem_space: Topdressing program drift, layering, and inconsistent surface response
first_checks: Review sand source consistency, interval discipline, and application integration
Inspect the profile for new discontinuities or layering near the surface
Compare firmness, moisture behavior, and organic-matter trend against the intended program
confirmatory_signs: Surface behavior changes after sand source or cadence changes
Profile inspection shows new layering or inconsistent incorporation
Softness and moisture inconsistency return even though topdressing is still happening on paper
avoid_assumptions: Do not assume the program is sound just because topdressing events are logged.
Do not blame weather alone if material drift and layering evidence are present.
topdressing_program_drift_framework.escalation: Treat it as a program-consistency problem and verify the physical profile before pushing more sand blindly.
